methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $2M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Nascent Materials is a seed-stage climate and energy company founded by Chaitanya Sharma that has raised $2.3M to date. The broader climate and energy sector comprises nine known private peers including Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBold Metals, and Electric Hydrogen, alongside one public comparable (Lianza, ticker LNZA). However, the absence of same-stage or adjacent-stage peers within the narrow climate and energy cohort—zero matches meeting strict stage and valuation-tier filters—limits peer-median applicability.

Stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable triangulation approach. Using the $2.3M total raised as a baseline proxy and applying a typical 2.5x multiplier for progression from seed to the next stage yields an implied valuation of $5.75M. This reflects standard step-up dynamics observed across comparable stage transitions in venture capital, without reference to company-specific operational or technical achievements.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $5M to $7M, with a median of $6M. This narrow range reflects reliance on a single quantitative method. Key uncertainties include the representativeness of a 2.5x multiplier for this specific company's risk profile and the broader climate and energy sector, as well as the absence of direct peer benchmarking data. A more robust triangulation would require identification of additional seed-stage comparables in the same sector or willingness to reference broader climate-tech datasets with acknowledged sector-specific adjustments.
